jokes.text: Two pair. A pair of red fives and a pair of black fives.
jokes.setup: Jazz claims to have 'two pair' in poker
jokes.punchline: Reveals four fives, which is actually four of a kind, not two pair
jokes.explanation: Clever misdirection playing on poker terminology. Jazz technically describes four of a kind as 'two pair' which is mathematically accurate but misleading.

jokes.text: According to my calculations, over the last three years... Jazz has beaten us for approximately $13,000.
jokes.setup: Carlton presenting precise mathematical analysis of their poker losses
jokes.punchline: The absurdly high amount lost to Jazz over time
jokes.explanation: Very Carlton-like to have exact calculations. Sets up the running gag about Jazz's poker dominance.

jokes.text: No? Where were you, Kalamazoo? Alabama.
jokes.setup: Jazz sarcastically suggests ridiculous location
jokes.punchline: Will's serious, unexpected answer
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective misdirection. Jazz expects denial, gets unexpected confirmation.

jokes.text: I witnessed a murder. [SINGING THE 'FRESH PRINCE OF BEL-AIR' THEME]
jokes.setup: Will drops bombshell about witnessing murder
jokes.punchline: Show's theme song plays immediately after, creating absurd juxtaposition
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-humor. The jarring contrast between serious revelation and upbeat theme song is perfectly timed comedy.

jokes.text: Ain't nothing that me and Will don't know about each other. Except this.
jokes.setup: Jazz claims he and Will know everything about each other
jokes.punchline: Immediately contradicts himself
jokes.explanation: Classic self-contradiction setup. Jazz undermines his own statement within the same breath.

jokes.text: You all got different styles. My mom usually like to cook with her clothes on.
jokes.setup: Laqueesha asks if Will is a better cook than his mom
jokes.punchline: Will's overshare about his mom's cooking habits
jokes.explanation: Classic Will Smith TMI moment. The casual delivery of inappropriate information creates awkward humor.

jokes.text: See, my moms won the WDAS-FM Back To Africa Contest. She spotted anything culturally wrong in The Lion King.
jokes.setup: Explaining why his mom is in Africa
jokes.punchline: Absurd contest premise about finding cultural issues in Disney movie
jokes.explanation: Clever satirical jab at both radio contests and Disney's cultural representation. Feels authentically 90s.

jokes.text: Of course, Curtis sees them all the time. He works at the post office.
jokes.setup: Will talking about seeing dead bodies for the first time
jokes.punchline: Casual reference to postal worker violence stereotype
jokes.explanation: References 90s 'going postal' stereotype. Dated humor that relies on cultural shorthand rather than original wit.

jokes.text: Duke told them, 'Over my dead body.' I sure do miss him.
jokes.setup: Duke's defiant statement to the mob
jokes.punchline: The ironic outcome that Duke's words were prophetic
jokes.explanation: Dark irony delivered with Will's characteristic casual tone. The juxtaposition works well.

jokes.text: Boy, your choking days is over. You're going to jail, fool.
jokes.setup: Will confronting the killer through glass
jokes.punchline: Overly confident trash talk from someone safely behind glass
jokes.explanation: Typical Will bravado when he feels safe. Character-consistent but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: Floyd Palmer? Hey, look, no. Uh-uh. Come on. I'm more like a Malcolm Akbar Mustafa. It's taken.
jokes.setup: Agent suggests Floyd Palmer as Will's new identity
jokes.punchline: Will rejects generic name for overly elaborate one that's already taken
jokes.explanation: Very Will-like to reject a boring identity for something more grandiose. The deadpan 'It's taken' is perfect.

jokes.text: Well, the nearest town is Hatred, Mississippi. I can check if there's dancing.
jokes.setup: Will wants to go out to clubs
jokes.punchline: Agent casually mentions nearby town called 'Hatred' and offers to check for dancing
jokes.explanation: Brilliant juxtaposition - the ominous town name 'Hatred' contrasted with the agent's helpful tone about checking for dancing.

jokes.text: Of course I love you, Laqueesha. I don't think your butt is too saggy.
jokes.setup: Will talking in his sleep
jokes.punchline: Inappropriate dream conversation about girlfriend's body
jokes.explanation: Basic sleep-talking humor. The specific content is mildly funny but relies on crude relationship humor.

jokes.text: Tough break. Your full house would have beaten my seven high.
jokes.setup: Jazz folds out of the poker hand
jokes.punchline: Will reveals Jazz would have won with a much better hand
jokes.explanation: Standard poker bluff reveal, competent but not particularly creative.

jokes.text: What life? I'm a married man. By the way, if Jewel asks, we were at our pottery class tonight.
jokes.setup: Will warns Jazz his life could be in danger
jokes.punchline: Jazz dismisses concern about his life, then immediately reveals he's lying to his wife
jokes.explanation: Good character moment for Jazz - simultaneously self-deprecating and sneaky. The pottery class alibi is specifically funny.

jokes.text: Will, son, I'll tell you what I'm doing here. You see, years ago I fell in love with... and married a wonderful woman named Vivian. Vivian had a sister named Vy. Vy gave birth to a child named Will... whose sole purpose in life has been to make mine a living hell.
jokes.setup: Uncle Phil explaining his presence, starting romantically
jokes.punchline: Escalates to blaming Will for ruining his life
jokes.explanation: Perfect Uncle Phil moment - starts with genuine sentiment, builds to comedic exasperation. Great structure and character voice.

jokes.text: whose sole purpose in life has been to make mine a living hell.
jokes.setup: Phil explaining his relationship to Will
jokes.punchline: Dramatically overstated assessment of Will's life purpose
jokes.explanation: Perfect Phil character moment - his tendency to be overly dramatic and verbose, great integration.

jokes.text: Is that a toupee? Yes, it's a toupee. It's a part of my new identity.
jokes.setup: Will notices Uncle Phil's appearance change
jokes.punchline: Phil's defeated admission about the toupee
jokes.explanation: Visual gag enhanced by Phil's resigned delivery. The image of dignified Uncle Phil in a bad toupee is inherently funny.

jokes.text: Where's the bathroom? It's right through that door. It leads outside.
jokes.setup: Normal request for bathroom location
jokes.punchline: Reveals primitive living conditions
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective revelation of how rough their living situation is. Good economy of words.

jokes.text: You better take this broomstick. There's something living in there.
jokes.setup: Already established the bathroom is outside
jokes.punchline: Reveals it's even worse than expected - dangerous
jokes.explanation: Good escalation - not only is the bathroom outside, it's potentially dangerous. The broomstick as weapon is absurd.

jokes.text: Carlton? There's no Carlton here. Thanks to you, Carlton Banks no longer exists. He's now Little Zeke from Cabin Creek.
jokes.setup: Will addressing Carlton by his real name
jokes.punchline: Carlton's dramatic rejection of his identity, new ridiculous name
jokes.explanation: Perfect Carlton drama over his new identity. The contrast between 'Carlton Banks' and 'Little Zeke from Cabin Creek' is hilarious.

jokes.text: I hate to say it, Carlton, but you and your family are spoiled. Sure, there was a homicidal maniac looking for you all... but you still got a free vacation on the government's tip.
jokes.setup: Will commenting on their situation
jokes.punchline: Reframes witness protection as a 'free vacation' despite danger
jokes.explanation: Good Will perspective - finding the silver lining in a dangerous situation. Character-appropriate optimism.

jokes.text: Uncle Phil, how you gonna be mad at me? You always telling me to do the right thing. So? You never listened to me before, why did you have to do it now?
jokes.setup: Will defending his moral choice
jokes.punchline: Phil's exasperated response about Will finally listening at the worst time
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment. Phil's frustration that Will chose THIS moment to start listening to him is perfectly crafted and relatable.

jokes.text: Oh, can't. Tomorrow I start my new job at the manure plant.
jokes.setup: Will invites Phil fishing
jokes.punchline: Sophisticated Uncle Phil now works at a manure plant
jokes.explanation: Great character contrast - dignified lawyer Phil reduced to manual labor at worst possible job. The mental image is perfect.

jokes.text: I hear you'd like to go dancing.
jokes.setup: Earlier mention of checking for dancing in Hatred, Mississippi
jokes.punchline: Implication that this intimidating woman is the 'dancing' option
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback to the earlier dancing joke. The implication of what 'dancing' means in this context is disturbing and funny.

jokes.text: Dear Neiman or Marcus, whichever of you gets this first. I'm being held hostage by my own government. Please send my fall/winter catalog care of Betty Mae Swilly, Deliverance, Alabama.
jokes.setup: Hilary writing a letter during their witness protection
jokes.punchline: She treats government protection like being held hostage and prioritizes getting fashion catalogs
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hilary character moment - her obliviousness to danger and obsession with shopping, plus the absurd tone mismatch.

jokes.text: But, uh, the kiln blew up. No, Jazz, he... He busy picking vase particles out of his butt. Ow! Ow!
jokes.setup: Will covering for Jazz's lie about pottery class
jokes.punchline: Increasingly elaborate and physical alibi
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the pottery class lie with physical comedy elements. Will's commitment to helping Jazz is funny.

jokes.text: Wait a minute, you'll miss the best part of the story, where we meet the bear. The bear? Oh, pfft, the bear. Yeah.
jokes.setup: Will trying to keep Jazz interested in the story
jokes.punchline: Casually mentions a bear encounter, others react with surprise
jokes.explanation: Good story escalation technique. The casual mention of a bear encounter adds absurdity to their tale.

jokes.text: I'm tired. How long have we been walking? I don't know. About three, four minutes.
jokes.setup: Carlton complaining about being tired from hiking
jokes.punchline: They've only been walking for minutes
jokes.explanation: Perfect Carlton moment - his privileged lifestyle showing through. The timing of the reveal is well-crafted.

jokes.text: Will Floyd, you ever been with a big woman? I was in the Statue of Liberty once.
jokes.setup: Bertha asking if Will has experience with 'big women'
jokes.punchline: Will's literal interpretation about being inside a large statue
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection joke. Will's completely innocent literal interpretation of 'big woman' is perfectly delivered and very quotable.

jokes.text: What is that on your teeth? Pudding.
jokes.setup: Will noticing something on Bertha's teeth
jokes.punchline: Bertha's casual response about having food on her teeth
jokes.explanation: Basic gross-out humor. Functional but not particularly clever or character-specific.

jokes.text: Our neighbor Sue Ellen was cooking something she had run over. Sorry I'm late, but it was so beautiful I just couldn't leave the marina. Chateaubriand? Mmm!
jokes.setup: Description of roadkill dinner
jokes.punchline: Aunt Viv treating it like fine dining
jokes.explanation: Good contrast between Aunt Viv's sophisticated attitude and the crude reality. The French cuisine reference adds to the absurdity.

jokes.text: There he is, Daddy. There's the dirty varmint made me pregnant. Hey, hey, wait a minute. I ain't make nobody pregnant.
jokes.setup: Will's romantic troubles escalating
jokes.punchline: Pregnancy accusation against Will
jokes.explanation: Predictable sitcom escalation. The shotgun wedding setup is familiar but works within the episode's logic.

jokes.text: [WHISPERS] Oh, damn witness protection program.
jokes.setup: Will's cover being blown by someone who thinks he's been there for years
jokes.punchline: Will realizing the witness protection program's backstory is causing problems
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the main premise. Will's whispered realization shows how the protection program is backfiring.

jokes.text: If you register at Tiffany's ask for Richard.
jokes.setup: Hillbilly patriarch arranging shotgun wedding
jokes.punchline: Casually mentions upscale wedding registry
jokes.explanation: Brilliant contrast - backwoods hillbilly casually referencing luxury department store. The specificity of 'ask for Richard' is perfect.

jokes.text: Me.
jokes.setup: Will saying any man would be proud to father Bertha's child
jokes.punchline: Carlton's immediate, flat contradiction
jokes.explanation: Perfect Carlton timing and delivery. Single word destroys Will's diplomatic statement. Maximum economy.

jokes.text: And at least I have this picture of you to keep in my drawers.
jokes.setup: Bertha talking about keeping Will's picture
jokes.punchline: Double entendre about where she'll keep it
jokes.explanation: Standard innuendo that works for the character. The double meaning is clear but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: Hatfield didn't use deodorant.
jokes.setup: Will realizing something as they plan the confrontation
jokes.punchline: Random, uncomfortable personal detail about his ally
jokes.explanation: Random gross-out observation that adds to the primitive setting. Functional but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: And then there was Posse which got killed at the box office.
jokes.setup: Will thinking about Western movie outcomes
jokes.punchline: References both actual movie violence and box office failure
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay connecting movie violence with commercial failure. Shows Will's pop culture awareness.

jokes.text: If you were me, you'd be good-looking.
jokes.setup: O'Neill threatening Will to run
jokes.punchline: Will's cocky response about his appearance
jokes.explanation: Perfect Will Smith cockiness in the face of danger. The logic is absurd but delivered with complete confidence.

jokes.text: If you were me, you'd be good-looking.
jokes.setup: Killer says 'If I were you, I would run'
jokes.punchline: Will's cocky comeback about being good-looking
jokes.explanation: Perfect Will Smith character moment - staying cocky even when facing death. Great structure and economy.

jokes.text: [IMITATING O'NEILL] 'I'm really going to enjoy squeezing the life out of you.'
jokes.setup: O'Neill makes a death threat
jokes.punchline: Will mocks him by doing an impression right to his face
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Will's complete inability to take threats seriously, typical confrontational humor.

jokes.text: Because I got something that you don't have. Family. What are you talking about? I got a sister in New Jersey.
jokes.setup: Will's dramatic buildup about having something O'Neill doesn't
jokes.punchline: O'Neill's mundane response deflating the moment
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of Will's dramatic moment. O'Neill's practical response undermines the cinematic buildup.

jokes.text: What are you talking about? I got a sister in New Jersey.
jokes.setup: Will says he has something O'Neill doesn't - family
jokes.punchline: O'Neill matter-of-factly mentions his own family member
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of Will's dramatic moment with deadpan practicality, humanizes the villain unexpectedly.

jokes.text: It was obvious the McCoys didn't know when to say when.
jokes.setup: Implication that the McCoys got too drunk during the confrontation
jokes.punchline: Will's understated assessment of their condition
jokes.explanation: Mild humor explaining why Will's backup plan failed. The phrase 'know when to say when' is appropriately folksy.

jokes.text: Say, my man, you mind if I run inside and put on a big pot of coffee?
jokes.setup: Will supposedly facing mortal danger from O'Neill
jokes.punchline: Politely asking the killer if he can make coffee
jokes.explanation: Absurd politeness in a life-threatening situation. Will's casual hospitality toward his would-be killer is perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: Mister, consider yourself hustled.
jokes.setup: Jazz realizing the whole story was fake
jokes.punchline: Will's triumphant admission of the con
jokes.explanation: Satisfying reveal that the whole story was a poker hustle. Good payoff for the episode's frame story.

jokes.text: Why don't you come over for some poker tomorrow... we'll tell you about Carlton and the alien.
jokes.setup: Will inviting Jazz back for more poker
jokes.punchline: Teasing an even more absurd story for next time
jokes.explanation: Good setup for potential future episodes. The alien story promises even more absurdity than the witness protection tale.

jokes.text: Now, if you'll excuse me, I have to go sit on a vase and get home to my wife.
jokes.setup: Jazz leaving after being hustled
jokes.punchline: Reference to the pottery class alibi he needs to maintain
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the pottery class deception. Jazz has to commit to his alibi even after being conned.

jokes.text: Hey, good night, Little Zeke. [IN SOUTHERN ACCENT] Good night, Will Floyd.
jokes.setup: Will and Carlton saying goodnight
jokes.punchline: They maintain their fake witness protection identities
jokes.explanation: Nice callback maintaining the fake identities even after the story is revealed as fiction. Carlton's accent attempt is character-appropriate.

jokes.text: [WILL SCREAMING] That will teach him to play with me.
jokes.setup: Will apparently being attacked
jokes.punchline: Carlton's satisfied reaction suggesting he pranked Will
jokes.explanation: Standard sitcom ending with physical comedy. Carlton getting revenge for being hustled provides symmetry but isn't particularly clever.
